name: Apple
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Pizza
notes: On a normal snack portion recite Mezonot and Al HaMichya. If eaten as a bread-like meal or in a quantity that satisfies, it requires Hamotzi and Birkat HaMazon; ask a rabbi.

name: Coffee
notes: Recite Shehakol before. Borei Nefashot is said after only if a revi'it is drunk in the normal manner.

name: Orange
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Pear
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Peach
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Nectarine
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Plum
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Apricot
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Cherry
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Mango
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Lemon
notes: A lemon is a tree fruit, so one says Borei Pri HaEtz when eating it on its own. A beracha acharona (Borei Nefashot) is said only if a full kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Lime
notes: A tree fruit; when eaten on its own recite Borei Pri HaEtz, and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Grapefruit
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Tangerine
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Persimmon
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Guava
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Lychee
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Kiwi
notes: A standard tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Coconut
notes: Coconut flesh is Borei Pri HaEtz by the accepted Sephardic ruling, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit. Coconut water is a separate question and is generally Shehakol.

name: Papaya
notes: The common Sephardic practice is to recite Borei Pri HaEtz on papaya, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit, though some treat it as HaAdama.

name: Pomegranate
notes: One of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before, and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Fig
notes: One of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before, and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Date
notes: One of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before, and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Olive
notes: One of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before, and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Green Grapes
notes: Grapes are one of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit. Grape juice or wine has its own beracha.

name: Red Grapes
notes: Grapes are one of the seven species; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Al HaEtz (me'ein shalosh) after a kezayit. Grape juice or wine has its own beracha.

name: Banana
notes: Although it grows on a tall plant, the banana plant is not a tree halachically, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Pineapple
notes: Pineapple grows on a low ground plant, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, not HaEtz, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Watermelon
notes: Watermelon grows on a ground vine, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Cantaloupe
notes: Melons grow on a ground vine, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Honeydew Melon
notes: Melons grow on a ground vine, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Strawberry
notes: Strawberries grow on a low annual plant, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Blueberry
notes: Blueberries grow on a perennial bush and are Borei Pri HaEtz by common Sephardic practice, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Raspberry
notes: Raspberries grow on a perennial cane and are treated as Borei Pri HaEtz by common Sephardic practice, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Blackberry
notes: Blackberries grow on a perennial cane and are treated as Borei Pri HaEtz by common Sephardic practice, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Avocado
notes: Avocado is a tree fruit; recite Borei Pri HaEtz before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Passion Fruit
notes: Passion fruit grows on a woody perennial vine and is Borei Pri HaEtz, with Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Carrot
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama whether raw or cooked.

name: Cucumber
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Tomato
notes: Although botanically a fruit, halacha treats the tomato as Borei Pri HaAdama since it grows on a soft annual plant.

name: Lettuce
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama. Check carefully for insects before eating.

name: Onion
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Bell Pepper
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Broccoli
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama. Check carefully for insects.

name: Cauliflower
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama. Check carefully for insects.

name: Peas
notes: A legume that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Green Beans
notes: A legume that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Corn
notes: Fresh corn on the cob or kernels grow in the ground and are Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Potato
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama, whether boiled or baked.

name: Sweet Potato
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Zucchini
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Eggplant
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Cabbage
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Celery
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Mushroom
notes: Mushrooms do not draw their nourishment from the ground, so the beracha is Shehakol, not HaAdama.

name: Garlic
notes: When eaten on its own, garlic is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Squash
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Spinach
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama. Check carefully for insects.

name: Radish
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Beet
notes: A vegetable that grows in the ground; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Kale
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama. Check carefully for insects.

name: Asparagus
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Okra
notes: A vegetable; the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: French Fries
notes: Even though fried, French fries are still recognizably potato and remain Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Mashed Potatoes
notes: Mashed potato remains Borei Pri HaAdama even though its form has changed.

name: Roasted Vegetables
notes: A mix of roasted vegetables that grow in the ground is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Vegetable Soup
notes: When eating the vegetables, recite Borei Pri HaAdama. If it is mostly broth and the vegetables are secondary, some recite Shehakol on the liquid; ask a rabbi.

name: Potato Chips
notes: Many Sephardic poskim hold potato chips remain Borei Pri HaAdama since the potato is still recognizable; some are stringent. Borei Nefashot only on a kezayit.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Yaprak)
notes: Grape leaves stuffed with rice and vegetables: when the rice is the main component some recite Mezonot, but when treated as a vegetable dish the common practice is HaAdama. Ask a rabbi if unsure.

name: Fried Cauliflower
notes: The cauliflower remains the primary food, so the beracha is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: Corn Snack Puffs
notes: On highly processed puffed corn snacks where the corn has lost its original form, many recite Shehakol; some hold HaAdama. Ask a rabbi if unsure.

name: Ratatouille
notes: A cooked stew of eggplant, zucchini, peppers and tomato is Borei Pri HaAdama.

name: White Bread
notes: Bread from the five grains; requires netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon.

name: Whole Wheat Bread
notes: Bread from the five grains; requires netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon.

name: Pita Bread
notes: Bread from the five grains; requires netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon.

name: Baguette
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Challah
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Bagel
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Dinner Roll
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Rye Bread
notes: Rye is one of the five grains; this is true bread. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Sourdough Bread
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Laffa
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Ciabatta
notes: Bread from one of the five grains. Wash netilat yadayim, recite Hamotzi, and say Birkat HaMazon afterward on a kezayit.

name: Cake
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Cookies
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Crackers
notes: Made from the five grains; normally Mezonot and Al HaMichya. If eaten as a full meal in bread-like quantity, consult a rabbi, as it may require Hamotzi and Birkat HaMazon.

name: Pretzels
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Pasta
notes: Made from durum wheat, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Spaghetti
notes: Made from wheat, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Noodles
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Oatmeal
notes: Made from oats, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Breakfast Cereal
notes: When made from the five grains (wheat, oats, etc.), Mezonot before and Al HaMichya after a kezayit. Note: corn or rice cereals differ (HaAdama/Shehakol before, Borei Nefashot after); check the grain.

name: Granola
notes: Primarily oats, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit of grain is eaten within the time.

name: Muffin
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Croissant
notes: Pas haba'ah b'kisnin made with fat-enriched dough; Mezonot and Al HaMichya. If eaten as a full bread-like meal, consult a rabbi, as it may require Hamotzi and Birkat HaMazon.

name: Pancakes
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Waffles
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Pie Crust
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit of crust is eaten within the time.

name: Matzah
notes: Outside a meal, matzah eaten as a snack is treated by many as Mezonot/Al HaMichya, but when set as a meal it is Hamotzi/Birkat HaMazon. At the Pesach Seder it is Hamotzi. Consult a rabbi for your situation.

name: Bourekas
notes: Dough made from the five grains; normally Mezonot and Al HaMichya. If eaten as a full meal in bread-like quantity, consult a rabbi.

name: Baklava
notes: Phyllo dough from wheat, one of the five grains, is the primary component; Mezonot before. Recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit of dough is eaten within the time.

name: Donut
notes: Fried sweet dough from wheat, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Couscous
notes: Made from semolina wheat, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Bulgur
notes: Parboiled cracked wheat, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Plain Rice
notes: Sephardic practice: Mezonot before, but Borei Nefashot after (not Al HaMichya), since rice is not one of the five grains.

name: Fried Rice
notes: Sephardic practice: Mezonot before, but Borei Nefashot after (not Al HaMichya), since rice is not one of the five grains. The rice is generally the primary food, so it covers mixed-in egg or vegetables.

name: Rice Cakes
notes: Sephardic practice: Mezonot before, but Borei Nefashot after (not Al HaMichya), since rice is not one of the five grains.

name: Basmati Rice
notes: Sephardic practice: Mezonot before, but Borei Nefashot after (not Al HaMichya), since rice is not one of the five grains.

name: Corn Chips
notes: Made from corn; Borei Pri HaAdama before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit. Some hold Shehakol since the corn is significantly processed; consult a rabbi if uncertain.

name: Popcorn
notes: Made from corn kernels; Borei Pri HaAdama before and Borei Nefashot after a kezayit.

name: Pretzel Snack Bites
notes: Made from wheat flour, one of the five grains. Mezonot before; recite Al HaMichya only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Wheat Crackers (Snack)
notes: Made from the five grains; normally Mezonot and Al HaMichya as a snack (pas haba'ah b'kisnin). If eaten as a full meal in bread-like quantity, consult a rabbi.

name: Rice Snack Crackers
notes: Sephardic practice: Mezonot before, but Borei Nefashot after (not Al HaMichya), since rice is not one of the five grains.

name: Water
notes: Shehakol is recited on water only when drinking to quench thirst, not merely to wash down food. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Sparkling Water
notes: Shehakol on carbonated water. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Cola
notes: Shehakol on soft drinks. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Soda
notes: Shehakol on soft drinks. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Lemonade
notes: Shehakol because it is water flavored with lemon and sugar, not pure squeezed fruit juice. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Iced Tea
notes: Shehakol on iced tea. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Tea
notes: Shehakol on tea. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the required time.

name: Herbal Tea
notes: Shehakol on herbal infusions. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Hot Chocolate
notes: Shehakol on hot chocolate. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Milkshake
notes: Shehakol and Borei Nefashot apply. If the milkshake is thick and eaten with a spoon rather than drunk, the acharona reckoning of revi'it within the time may not be met; ask a rabbi for unclear cases.

name: Smoothie
notes: A smoothie made of blended whole fruit may require Haetz or Haadama (not Shehakol) if the fruit pieces are still recognizable. Shehakol applies when fully liquefied into juice. Ask a rabbi for unclear cases.

name: Energy Drink
notes: Shehakol on energy and sports drinks. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Beer
notes: Although made from barley, beer is Shehakol since the grain is not eaten as such. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Whiskey
notes: Shehakol on liquor. Borei Nefashot is generally not recited after distilled spirits because a revi'it is rarely drunk at once; ask a rabbi for unclear cases.

name: Almond Milk
notes: Plant-based milk substitute is Shehakol. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Soy Milk
notes: Plant-based milk substitute is Shehakol. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Oat Milk
notes: Although made from oats, oat milk is Shehakol since it is a thin beverage and the grain is not eaten as such. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Orange Juice
notes: Juice squeezed from fruit (other than grapes) is Shehakol, not Haetz. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Apple Juice
notes: Juice squeezed from fruit (other than grapes) is Shehakol, not Haetz. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Pomegranate Juice
notes: Juice squeezed from fruit (other than grapes) is Shehakol, not Haetz, even though the pomegranate is one of the seven species. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Decaf Coffee
notes: Shehakol on coffee. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the required time.

name: Latte
notes: Both the milk and the coffee are Shehakol, so a single Shehakol covers the whole drink. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Cappuccino
notes: Both the milk and the coffee are Shehakol. Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Wine
notes: Wine has its own beracha, Borei Peri HaGefen, and Al HaGefen afterward when a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Grape Juice
notes: Grape juice, unlike other fruit juices, takes Borei Peri HaGefen and Al HaGefen when a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Sparkling Wine
notes: Sparkling wine takes Borei Peri HaGefen and Al HaGefen when a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Milk
notes: Milk drunk as a beverage is Shehakol; Borei Nefashot only if a revi'it is drunk within the time.

name: Cheese
notes: Shehakol on cheese. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Hard Cheese
notes: Shehakol on hard cheese. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Cottage Cheese
notes: Shehakol on cottage cheese. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Cream Cheese
notes: Shehakol on cream cheese. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Yogurt
notes: Plain yogurt is Shehakol. Fruit-on-the-bottom or fruit-mixed yogurt may warrant a separate beracha on a significant piece of fruit; ask a rabbi for unclear cases. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Butter
notes: Butter eaten on its own is Shehakol. When spread on bread it is covered by Hamotzi. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Sour Cream
notes: Shehakol on sour cream. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Ice Cream
notes: Ice cream is Shehakol. Because it melts and is partly swallowed as liquid, some require a revi'it for Borei Nefashot while others reckon a kezayit; ask a rabbi for unclear cases.

name: Whipped Cream
notes: Shehakol on whipped cream. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Chicken
notes: Shehakol on poultry. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Roast Chicken
notes: Shehakol on poultry. Borei Nefashot only if a kezayit is eaten within the time.

name: Grilled Chicken
notes: Poultry is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Beef
notes: Meat is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Steak
notes: Meat is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Hamburger Patty
notes: A plain meat patty without a bun is shehakol and Borei Nefashot. Eaten inside a real bread bun, the bread becomes the ikar and the beracha is HaMotzi.

name: Lamb
notes: Meat is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Turkey
notes: Poultry is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Salami
notes: Cured meat is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Hot Dog
notes: A plain hot dog sausage without a bun is shehakol and Borei Nefashot. With a real bread bun, the bread is the ikar and the beracha is HaMotzi.

name: Salmon
notes: Fish is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Tuna
notes: Fish is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Gefilte Fish
notes: Ground fish is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Sardines
notes: Fish is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Fried Fish
notes: Plain fried fish is shehakol and Borei Nefashot. A thick batter coating eaten for its own sake may change the beracha; consult a rabbi.

name: Eggs
notes: Eggs are shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Hard Boiled Egg
notes: Eggs are shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Omelette
notes: Eggs are shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot. If vegetables are a significant added component, they may warrant their own beracha; ask a rabbi.

name: Scrambled Eggs
notes: Eggs are shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Chocolate
notes: Chocolate is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Dark Chocolate
notes: Chocolate is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Candy
notes: Candy is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Lollipop
notes: A boiled-sugar sweet is shehakol; Borei Nefashot is recited only if a kezayit is consumed within the time, which is uncommon for a lollipop.

name: Gummy Candy
notes: Gummy candy is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Pudding
notes: Pudding is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Jello
notes: Gelatin dessert is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Halva
notes: Sesame halva is shehakol (sesame is not one of the five grains), followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Marshmallow
notes: Marshmallow is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Sorbet
notes: Although fruit-flavored, sorbet is a processed frozen dessert and the beracha is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot.

name: Honey
notes: Honey eaten on its own is shehakol. When used to dip or sweeten another food, that food's beracha applies.

name: Sugar
notes: Sugar eaten on its own is shehakol, followed by Borei Nefashot on a kezayit.

name: Cheesecake
notes: Mixed-component dessert: the grain crust is mezonot while the cheese filling is shehakol. The common practice treats the mezonot crust as primary, saying Mezonot and Al HaMichya on a kezayit of crust. Ask a rabbi for your specific case.

name: Tiramisu
notes: Mixed-component dessert: grain-based ladyfingers (mezonot) layered with mascarpone cream (shehakol). The grain component is generally primary, so Mezonot and Al HaMichya on a kezayit of cake. Ask a rabbi for your specific case.

name: Brownie
notes: A baked flour-based dessert from the five grains is mezonot, followed by Al HaMichya on a kezayit.

name: Apple Pie
notes: The grain pie crust is mezonot and is generally the ikar, so Mezonot and Al HaMichya on a kezayit of crust. The apple filling on its own would be haetz. Ask a rabbi for your specific case.

name: Fruit Salad
notes: A mix of tree fruits (haetz) and ground fruits like melon (haadama). When haetz fruits are present, recite haetz; recite haadama separately on the ground fruits if they are not secondary. Borei Nefashot is the beracha acharona on a kezayit (unless one of the five special fruits is the focus, which is Al HaEtz). Ask a rabbi for a specific mix.

name: Chocolate-Covered Strawberry
notes: Mixed-component dessert: the strawberry (haadama) is the primary food with the chocolate (shehakol) as a coating, so the beracha is haadama, followed by Borei Nefashot. Ask a rabbi for your specific case.

name: Sandwich (on bread)
notes: Made with real bread, the bread is the ikar and covers the filling. Wash netilat yadayim with a beracha, say HaMotzi, and recite Birkat HaMazon afterward if a kezayit of bread was eaten.

name: Sub / Hero Sandwich
notes: Built on a real bread roll, so the bread is primary. Requires netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon. The meat, cheese, and vegetables are secondary to the bread.

name: Hamburger (with bun)
notes: Eaten inside a real bread bun, the bread is the ikar. Wash netilat yadayim, say HaMotzi, and recite Birkat HaMazon. A patty eaten alone without a bun would be shehakol/nefashot.

name: Hot Dog (in bun)
notes: The bun is real bread and is the ikar; the sausage is secondary. Requires netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon. A hot dog eaten without a bun is shehakol/nefashot.

name: Wrap (flour tortilla)
notes: A soft flour tortilla used as bread to enclose a meal is generally treated as bread (HaMotzi, netilat yadayim, Birkat HaMazon). Some hold a thin tortilla is mezonot when eaten as a snack; when eaten as a meal-sized portion treat as bread. Consult a rav for borderline cases.

name: Pita Sandwich
notes: Standard pita is real bread, so the pita is the ikar. Wash netilat yadayim, say HaMotzi, and recite Birkat HaMazon on a kezayit. The fillings are covered by the bread's beracha.

name: Falafel in Pita
notes: When served inside a real pita or laffa, the bread is the ikar: netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon. Falafel balls eaten plain (without bread) are haadama, since chickpeas are a ground-growing legume.

name: Sushi
notes: By the Sephardic ruling, cooked rice is mezonot, and in sushi the rice is the ikar, so the beracha is mezonot before and nefashot after (not al hamichya, since rice is not one of the five grains). The fish, seaweed (nori), and vegetables are secondary to the rice. If one eats a piece that is essentially only fish, that piece is shehakol.

name: Schnitzel (breaded cutlet)
notes: The meat or chicken is the ikar and the breadcrumb coating is secondary (it serves the meat as a coating for flavor and texture), so the beracha is shehakol before and nefashot after. The breading does not change the beracha because it is not eaten for its own sake.

name: Cereal with Milk
notes: If the cereal is made from one of the five grains (wheat, oats, etc.), the cereal is the ikar: mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit eaten within the time limit). The milk is secondary. Corn flakes and rice cereals are not from the five grains, so they would be shehakol/nefashot or borderline; check the ingredients and consult a rav.

name: Chicken Soup with Noodles
notes: If there is a meaningful amount of noodles (made from wheat) in the soup, the noodles are treated as the ikar and the beracha is mezonot; al hamichya is said after if a kezayit of noodles is eaten within the time limit. If the noodles are minimal and the broth is the ikar, the beracha is shehakol/nefashot. The exact ruling depends on the proportion.

name: Chicken Soup with Rice
notes: When rice is a significant component, the rice is the ikar (cooked rice is mezonot per the Sephardic rule), so the beracha is mezonot before and nefashot after (not al hamichya, since rice is not a five-grain). If the rice is only a minor garnish and the broth is primary, the beracha would be shehakol/nefashot. Depends on proportion.

name: Minestrone (with pasta)
notes: If the soup contains a kezayit of pasta (wheat) and the pasta is a meaningful component, it is treated as mezonot, and al hamichya is said after if a kezayit is eaten in the time limit. If the vegetables and beans are clearly the ikar with only token pasta, the beracha may be haadama or shehakol. The proportions determine the ruling.

name: Salad with Croutons
notes: The vegetables are the ikar and the croutons are typically secondary garnish, so the beracha is haadama before and nefashot after. If one wishes to eat a significant amount of croutons as a food in their own right, one should also say mezonot on them (ideally on a separate crouton first). If the salad is primarily protein (eggs/tuna), it may be shehakol; depends on composition.

name: Yogurt with Granola
notes: This has two notable components. If the yogurt is the ikar and the granola is a topping, say shehakol on the yogurt (nefashot after). If one wants to eat a meaningful amount of granola (oats are a five-grain), it is best to say mezonot on a piece of granola first, then shehakol on the yogurt. When both are eaten together as primary, many recite both berachot to be safe; consult a rav.

name: Ice Cream Cone
notes: The ice cream is shehakol and is usually the ikar; the cone is mezonot but is generally considered a holder/secondary, so shehakol on the ice cream covers it. If one specifically wants to eat the cone as a food (e.g. a thick sugar cone enjoyed for its own sake), it is best to say mezonot on a bit of the cone first, then shehakol on the ice cream. Nefashot after.

name: Granola Bar
notes: If oats (a five-grain) are the ikar and bind the bar, the beracha is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit within the time limit). If the bar is mostly nuts, seeds, and dried fruit held together with honey or syrup and the grain is minor, the beracha may shift toward shehakol or follow the majority ingredient; read the ingredient list.

name: Stuffed Peppers (with rice)
notes: When the rice (and/or meat) filling is the main part eaten and the pepper mainly serves as a vessel, the rice filling is the ikar: mezonot before, nefashot after (cooked rice is mezonot per the Sephardic rule). If the pepper itself is a substantial part eaten for its own sake, haadama may apply. Many of these dishes are eaten as part of a bread meal, in which case HaMotzi covers them.

name: Calzone
notes: A calzone is a folded dough pocket with cheese/sauce filling. When eaten as a snack it is generally mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit). However, since it is essentially bread dough, eating a meal-sized amount or eating it as the basis of a meal can obligate HaMotzi, netilat yadayim, and Birkat HaMazon. Consult a rav for the quantity threshold.

name: Garlic Bread
notes: If made from regular bread (a sliced baguette or loaf) it remains bread: HaMotzi, netilat yadayim, and Birkat HaMazon on a kezayit. If it is made from a mezonot dough (enriched with significant oil/fruit juice rather than water) and eaten as a snack, it could be mezonot/al hamichya. Identify the type of dough to determine the beracha.

name: Malawach
notes: A Yemenite flaky fried dough, layered with margarine or oil. Because the dough is enriched and pan-fried (not classic baked bread), it is generally mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit). However, since it is a substantial dough food, eating a meal-sized portion or making a meal of it can obligate HaMotzi and Birkat HaMazon. Yemenite and Sephardic poskim discuss this; consult a rav.

name: Jachnun
notes: A Yemenite slow-baked rolled dough traditionally eaten Shabbat morning. The dough is rolled with oil/margarine and baked overnight. Many treat it as mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit) because of the enriched, layered nature. Some poskim, given its bread-like character and that it is often eaten as a meal, require HaMotzi and Birkat HaMazon. Practice varies; consult a rav.

name: Kibbeh / Kubbeh
notes: A signature Syrian/Iraqi-Kurdish dish: a bulgur (cracked wheat) shell filled with spiced meat, often in soup. The ruling depends on the ikar: if the bulgur shell is significant and the dish is eaten for the dumpling, mezonot may apply; if the meat filling and broth are primary and the shell is thin, shehakol applies. Listed here as shehakol/nefashot for thin-shell soup kibbeh; thicker fried kibbeh leans mezonot. Consult a rav.

name: Sambusak
notes: A half-moon Sephardic/Iraqi pastry of dough filled with cheese, chickpeas, or meat. The dough is the ikar, so the beracha is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit). As with all dough pastries, a meal-sized amount eaten as a meal could obligate HaMotzi; eaten as a snack it is mezonot.

name: Kubaneh
notes: A Yemenite slow-baked enriched bread eaten Shabbat morning. Despite being enriched with butter/oil, kubaneh is bread-like and commonly eaten as a meal, so many poskim require HaMotzi, netilat yadayim, and Birkat HaMazon. Some treat a small snack portion as mezonot due to the enriched dough. Practice varies between communities; consult a rav.

name: Mufleta
notes: A thin Moroccan pan-fried dough traditionally eaten at Mimouna (after Pesach) with honey and butter. Because it is a thin, pan-fried dough rather than baked bread, many treat it as mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit). Some hold it is bread-like if eaten in quantity as a meal, requiring HaMotzi. Moroccan minhag varies; consult a rav.

name: Lahmacun / Lahmajun
notes: A thin flatbread topped with spiced minced meat (Turkish/Syrian/Armenian). When the dough is thin and eaten as a snack, it is generally mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit), with the meat topping secondary. If made on a thicker bread base or eaten as a full meal, it may require HaMotzi and Birkat HaMazon. The thickness of the dough and quantity determine the ruling; consult a rav.

name: Ka'ak
notes: Ka'ak refers to crunchy sesame-coated ring biscuits/crackers common in Syrian and other Sephardic communities (distinct from the soft sesame bread sold on streets). The dry, sweet, snack version is typically mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit). A soft bread-type ka'ak eaten as bread would be HaMotzi. Identify which type; consult a rav for the soft variety.

name: Ma'amoul (date-filled)
notes: A Sephardic shortbread-type cookie (semolina/flour dough) filled with dates, walnuts, or pistachios. The dough is the ikar, so the beracha is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit of the grain). The date or nut filling is secondary and does not change the beracha. If made entirely without grain it would follow the filling, but the standard recipe is grain-based.

name: Sfiha / Lahem Bi Ajeen
notes: A small open-faced Syrian meat pastry on a dough base. As a snack-sized pastry the dough is the ikar and the beracha is mezonot (al hamichya after a kezayit). If the base is genuine bread or eaten as a full meal, HaMotzi may apply. Depends on dough type and quantity; consult a rav.

name: Mujadara
notes: A classic dish of rice (or bulgur) cooked with lentils and fried onions. If rice is the majority, the rice is the ikar: mezonot before, nefashot after (cooked rice is mezonot per the Sephardic rule). If made with bulgur (wheat) as the majority, it would be mezonot with al hamichya after. If lentils predominate, haadama may apply. The grain-to-lentil ratio determines the beracha.

name: Kousa Mahshi (stuffed zucchini)
notes: Zucchini or squash hollowed and stuffed with a rice-and-meat filling, a staple of Syrian and Iraqi tables. When the rice filling is the main part eaten, the rice is the ikar: mezonot before, nefashot after. If the squash is eaten substantially for its own sake, haadama may apply. Often served within a bread meal, in which case HaMotzi covers it. Consult a rav for the standalone case.

name: Couscous with Vegetables
notes: Couscous is made from semolina (wheat, a five-grain), so it is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit within the time limit). When served with a vegetable or meat stew on top, the couscous is the ikar and the toppings are secondary. A primarily-vegetable plate with token couscous would follow the vegetables.

name: Shakshuka
notes: Eggs poached in a tomato and pepper sauce. The dish is generally shehakol (the egg is the ikar and is shehakol; even on the cooked tomato sauce, blended cooked vegetables often default to shehakol). Nefashot after. If eaten with bread dipped into it, the bread is HaMotzi and covers the dish. When the pepper/tomato are eaten as recognizable pieces for their own sake, haadama could apply; consult a rav.

name: Ptitim (Israeli couscous)
notes: Toasted wheat pasta shaped into small balls. Since it is made from wheat (a five-grain), the beracha is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit within the time limit), like any pasta. Toppings of vegetables or sauce are secondary when the ptitim is the ikar.

name: Burrito
notes: A large flour tortilla wrapped around rice, beans, meat, and fillings. A substantial flour tortilla eaten as a meal is widely treated as bread, requiring netilat yadayim, HaMotzi, and Birkat HaMazon. Some hold a thin tortilla is mezonot. Given the meal-sized portion, most treat the burrito as bread; consult a rav for the snack-sized or very thin-wrap case.

name: Stuffed Cabbage (with rice)
notes: Cabbage leaves rolled around a rice-and-meat filling. When the rice filling is the main part eaten and the cabbage is the wrapper, the rice is the ikar: mezonot before, nefashot after (cooked rice is mezonot per the Sephardic rule). If the cabbage is eaten substantially for its own sake, haadama may apply, and if the filling is mostly meat, shehakol. Often eaten in a bread meal, where HaMotzi covers it. Consult a rav for the standalone case.

name: Rice Pudding
notes: Cooked rice with milk and sugar. By the Sephardic rule cooked rice is mezonot, and here the rice is the ikar, so the beracha is mezonot before and nefashot after (not al hamichya, since rice is not a five-grain). The milk and sugar are secondary. If the dish is so liquid that the milk is the ikar, some would say shehakol; the standard thick pudding is mezonot.

name: Soup with Croutons / Crackers
notes: Croutons or crackers added to soup are made from a five-grain. If one eats a kezayit of them and they are a meaningful part of the dish, say mezonot (al hamichya after). If they are a minor garnish dissolved into the broth and the soup is the ikar, the soup's beracha (often shehakol or haadama) governs. To avoid doubt, some say mezonot on a crouton/cracker before adding it to the soup.

name: Chocolate-Covered Pretzel
notes: The pretzel (a five-grain dough) is the ikar and the chocolate coating is secondary, so the beracha is mezonot before and al hamichya after (on a kezayit of the pretzel within the time limit). If someone eats it specifically for the chocolate (e.g. a thin pretzel heavily enrobed in chocolate eaten as candy), some would say it is borderline; the standard ruling follows the pretzel as ikar.
